company.name: Monolithic Power Systems
company.description: Monolithic Power Systems designs and sells high-performance power management semiconductors and power modules for data-center, automotive, industrial, communications, consumer, and computing systems.

overview.title: Power-management semiconductor specialist
paragraphs: Monolithic Power Systems is a fabless semiconductor company focused on high-performance power electronics, including DC/DC converters, power modules, AC/DC products, motor drivers, power-management ICs, current-limit switches, and lighting-control products.

Its products are embedded inside customer systems rather than sold as end-user devices, so the business depends on design wins, application support, process know-how, distributor relationships, and reliable execution across many end markets.

overview.title: AI and data-center exposure
paragraphs: Recent filings and earnings materials show strong growth from enterprise data and communications customers, reflecting demand for power solutions used in AI servers, optical networking, storage, and related infrastructure.

That growth strengthens the strategic importance of efficient point-of-load power conversion, but it also ties valuation and customer concentration risk to large capital-spending cycles in data-center infrastructure.

moatNarrative: MPS has a meaningful moat because power-management chips are selected through engineering qualification, customer design cycles, reliability requirements, packaging constraints, and distributor/application-support workflows. A successful DC/DC regulator or module can stay in a platform for years once designed in.

The moat is not absolute. The company operates in competitive analog and power semiconductor markets where large peers, second sources, and customer redesigns can pressure pricing. Its 2025 annual report also highlights distributor concentration and the need to keep introducing new products with attractive margins.
decentralizationNarrative: MPS is only moderately vulnerable to decentralization because advanced power semiconductors still require specialized design talent, process integration, packaging, test infrastructure, and qualified manufacturing partners. Open designs can replace some board-level reference work, but they do not easily replicate silicon-level execution.

The most credible decentralizing pressure is around modular power-system design: open voltage-regulator standards, open charge-controller firmware, open energy hardware, and small-batch PCB assembly can make some application-specific power modules more interchangeable, repairable, and locally adaptable.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 126-150.

moat.rationale: High-performance power ICs and modules benefit from design-in inertia, reliability qualification, application engineering, packaging know-how, proprietary process integration, and broad product families, but the market remains competitive and customer redesigns can displace suppliers.

decentralizability.rationale: Board-level power electronics can be made more open and modular, but the core silicon, packaging, qualification, and high-volume manufacturing stack remains capital- and expertise-intensive.

profitability.rationale: The 2025 Form 10-K reports approximately $2.79 billion in revenue, $1.54 billion gross profit, about 55.2% gross margin, and $621 million in net income for fiscal 2025.

peRatio.rationale: StockAnalysis reported a trailing P/E ratio of 111.73 around May 21, 2026, making the valuation highly sensitive to continued growth expectations.

marketCap.rationale: StockAnalysis reported Monolithic Power Systems market capitalization at approximately $78.11 billion as of May 22, 2026.

products.name: DC/DC converters
products.summary: MPS DC/DC converter products convert and regulate voltage rails across systems such as servers, AI hardware, storage, notebooks, automotive electronics, appliances, networking equipment, and satellite communications.
products.whyItMatters: DC/DC conversion is a hidden dependency in nearly every electronic system: efficiency, transient response, board area, thermals, and reliability all affect whether downstream compute, communications, and energy hardware can operate at scale.
replacementSketch: The realistic replacement path is not a one-for-one open-source chip substitute. It starts with open regulator specifications, reusable reference designs, verified PCB layouts, and firmware-controlled commodity modules for lower-risk applications.
For high-current server and AI rails, open alternatives would more likely pressure integration and interoperability expectations than fully replace specialized silicon in the near term.

alternatives.name: OpenVReg
alternatives.summary: OpenVReg is an open voltage-regulator specification that standardizes package, pinout, and functionality for low-voltage DC/DC switching regulators.

openness.rationale: The specification is public and explicitly intended to standardize regulator interfaces, although implementations can still be proprietary.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Interface standardization can reduce vendor lock-in and make regulator modules more swappable, but it does not decentralize semiconductor fabrication itself.

readiness.rationale: OpenVReg is a concrete hardware specification, but broad availability depends on vendor adoption and compatible module ecosystems.

costLeverage.rationale: Standardized pinouts and functional expectations can improve second sourcing and reduce redesign cost, though silicon performance remains supplier-dependent.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open Regulator Qualification Network
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open hardware qualification network could publish tested DC/DC converter reference designs, regulator module footprints, thermal profiles, load-transient results, and failure reports so smaller hardware makers can reuse verified power stages without depending entirely on one vendor's application notes.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The concept shifts part of the power-converter moat from proprietary application support toward shared verification artifacts, making low- and mid-risk board-level power designs easier to source, audit, repair, and second-source.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization matters through shared test data and multi-lab verification rather than Bitcoin. Independent labs, makerspaces, contract manufacturers, and users could publish reproducible measurements against common load profiles.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Designers submit schematics, BOMs, layouts, test scripts, and measured results; independent labs rerun tests; manufacturers list compatible modules or boards; buyers select designs based on verified operating envelopes.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Test submissions would need raw logs, photos, thermal captures, instrument calibration metadata, BOM hashes, PCB revision hashes, and repeated results from independent labs. Reputation and signed result bundles reduce fake claims, but cannot fully prevent cherry-picked operating conditions.
failureModes: High-current and safety-critical applications may still require proprietary engineering support and formal qualification.
Open test results can be gamed if labs are not independent or if operating profiles omit real customer edge cases.
Component substitutions during shortages can invalidate a published design without obvious visual changes.
adoptionPath: Start with low-voltage maker, robotics, lab supply, and industrial-control boards where open test coverage is useful but certification barriers are manageable.
Add standardized load-transient, EMI, thermal, and derating test profiles for common voltage/current classes.
Let contract manufacturers and distributors publish verified build variants tied to exact BOM and PCB revisions.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The model distributes design validation and sourcing intelligence, but it still depends on centralized semiconductor fabs and component suppliers.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open hardware communities already publish schematics and regulator designs, but rigorous multi-lab electrical qualification would require stronger governance and tooling.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The test methods and hardware are feasible for modest power levels, while EMI, safety, and high-current transient testing raise cost and complexity.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This would pressure application-note lock-in and low-end module margins more than it would displace high-performance proprietary converter silicon.

products.name: Power modules
products.summary: MPS power modules integrate power-management ICs with surrounding components such as inductors to reduce design work, save board area, improve layout repeatability, and accelerate customer adoption.
products.whyItMatters: Power modules move value from discrete board design into compact integrated packages. That helps engineers ship faster, but it can also deepen dependency on vendor-specific module footprints, documentation, supply chains, and qualification assumptions.
replacementSketch: Open replacement pressure is most plausible in modular DC energy systems, lab supplies, solar charge controllers, and local hardware projects that can trade maximum density for transparency, repairability, and supply-chain flexibility.

For dense AI-server and data-center applications, open power modules are more likely to complement proprietary silicon through open carriers, monitoring firmware, and standardized interfaces than to replace advanced packaged modules immediately.

alternatives.name: Libre Solar
alternatives.summary: Libre Solar develops open hardware and open software for DC energy systems, including charge controllers, battery-management systems, and firmware for solar and battery applications.

openness.rationale: Libre Solar publishes hardware, firmware, documentation, and GitHub repositories for open DC energy-system components.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The project directly supports locally understandable and modifiable solar, battery, and DC power systems rather than closed appliance-style energy stacks.

readiness.rationale: Libre Solar has real designs and firmware, but some hardware pages describe inactive or evolving designs and the ecosystem is not a drop-in replacement for commercial high-density modules.

costLeverage.rationale: Open designs can reduce engineering lock-in and support local repair or small-batch builds, while component sourcing, certification, and assembly costs remain material.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open DC Energy Module Stack
disruptionConcepts.summary: Open DC energy modules could package charge control, battery management, telemetry, and load switching into auditable building blocks for homes, farms, workshops, and small facilities that want repairable local energy systems rather than closed proprietary boxes.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: This shifts competition away from opaque integrated modules toward modular, documented power blocks that local installers and communities can inspect, repair, and adapt for distributed energy systems.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: The decentralization role is practical energy sovereignty: users and local operators coordinate around shared open hardware, firmware, and operating profiles. Bitcoin is not central to the mechanism.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Design maintainers publish module revisions, firmware releases, and safety envelopes; local builders and installers assemble certified variants; users and community energy groups share operating data and maintenance notes.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust would come from reproducible schematics, firmware source, hardware revision identifiers, test logs, installation checklists, and independent safety reviews. Devices can report signed telemetry, but physical inspection and conservative derating remain essential.
failureModes: Certification, liability, and electrical-code compliance can block adoption in grid-connected or high-power installations.
Poorly assembled local modules could create fire, shock, or battery-safety hazards.
Open designs may lag proprietary modules in density, efficiency, thermal performance, and support availability.
adoptionPath: Begin with off-grid, educational, research, and makerspace DC systems where transparency and repairability matter more than maximum density.
Standardize module connectors, telemetry formats, derating rules, and test fixtures for common solar-plus-battery use cases.
Build installer and community-lab networks that can verify builds, publish failures, and maintain shared spare-parts pools.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept directly supports distributed energy generation, open energy hardware, and locally maintainable DC systems.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open hardware and firmware communities can coordinate revisions and field data, but certified installer networks and safety review processes would need to mature.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Low- and medium-power DC modules are feasible today, while high-power, grid-tied, or safety-critical deployments face certification and liability barriers.

incumbentPressure.rationale: Open DC modules can pressure proprietary integration in distributed energy and lower-power applications, but they are unlikely to displace dense commercial data-center power modules quickly.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Monolithic Power Systems bundle with two products: DC/DC converters and power modules. The refresh uses official MPS filings and product pages for the company/product profile, StockAnalysis for current market metrics, and OpenVReg plus Libre Solar as open/decentralized pressure points.
